title: Snubs, Surprises, and WTFs of the Golden Globe Nominations, With Mark Harris
format: Sean and Amanda pick through a baffling round of Golden Globe nominations, then Mark Harris joins to talk awards-season chaos and his new Mike Nichols biography.

films.title: The Little Things
films.note: Jared Leto's supporting-actor nod is, in Sean's words, the single biggest WTF of the whole slate — they liked him in a movie that 'was not a film that worked at all.'

films.title: Music
films.note: Sia's directorial debut, released by Vertical Entertainment, wasn't even out yet and had zero footprint online; Kate Hudson's Best Actress nod left both hosts stumped — neither had heard of it.

films.title: The Mauritanian
films.note: Kevin Macdonald's Guantanamo drama landed Jodie Foster and Tahar Rahim nominations for a movie almost nobody had seen yet — Sean calls Rahim's work genuinely good, the film itself 'fine,' a docudrama that 'skips over all the actual drama.'

films.title: The Prom
films.note: Ryan Murphy's Netflix musical got one nomination — James Corden, whom Sean names the worst performance in the movie, over Meryl Streep and Nicole Kidman.

films.title: Emma.
films.note: Anya Taylor-Joy's second nomination of the morning — 'a nice surprise,' per Amanda.

films.title: I Care a Lot
films.note: Rosamund Pike's third Globes nomination, for a Netflix film that premiered at TIFF and still hadn't landed with audiences.

films.title: The Personal History of David Copperfield
films.note: Dev Patel's nomination surprised Amanda, who didn't realize the Armando Iannucci film had finally hit VOD/Disney+; Sean found the movie itself disappointing.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: Two nominations including Best Musical or Comedy — a rare good surprise both hosts were happy about.

films.title: News of the World
films.note: Helena Zengel's nod was one of the film's only nominations; Tom Hanks himself was shut out entirely.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Largely shut out otherwise, but Ludwig Göransson's score got in — Sean calls it 'one of the great scores of the year.'

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: Spike Lee's film — not even his own top five, per the hosts, but still one of the year's best — got zero nominations, shutting out Delroy Lindo and Spike as director.

films.title: Malcolm & Marie
films.note: Zendaya got nothing for it, and the film itself was shut out entirely — a contrast Sean draws against Andra Day's nod for the far-less-famous United States vs. Billie Holiday.

films.title: The United States vs. Billie Holiday
films.note: Andra Day's Best Actress nomination struck Sean as an odd bid to court a younger audience, given Zendaya went unrecognized for Malcolm & Marie instead.

films.title: Let Them All Talk
films.note: One of two films, alongside The Prom, where Meryl Streep was passed over.

films.title: The Way Back
films.note: Ben Affleck's comeback drama got no nominations, despite the hosts' self-admitted 'unhealthy affection' for Affleck himself.

films.title: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
films.note: Viola Davis and Chadwick Boseman were nominated as expected, but the film got nothing else despite being a Best Picture front-runner.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: Just two nominations for a film Sean says he'd take over The Trial of the Chicago 7 '100 times out of 100' — yet Chicago 7 got the Best Picture Drama slot and Judas didn't.

films.title: One Night in Miami...
films.note: Regina King got a Best Director nod and Leslie Odom Jr. a supporting-actor nomination — real recognition, but low relative to the film's other stars.

films.title: Sound of Metal
films.note: Paul Raci went unrecognized, unsurprising for a small performance on a streamer, per Amanda.

films.title: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
films.note: Amanda calls it the best film released last year; it never had a chance at the Globes.

segments.heading: The Narratives
segments.note: what the noms say about where the season's headed

films.title: Mank
films.note: Led all nominations with six — a bump neither host fully trusts, tangled up with the film's Citizen Kane/Orson Welles baggage and muted Netflix word of mouth.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Held serve on its expected nominations, but Amanda flags a 'danger zone' of inflated expectations once general audiences actually see it.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Five nominations, a Paramount film sold to Netflix — one of six of the ten Best Picture/Musical nominees with streaming provenance, which the hosts call a real breakthrough.

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Four nominations including a Best Director nod for Emerald Fennell, one of three women in that category this year.

films.title: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm
films.note: Maria Bakalova and Sacha Baron Cohen both nominated, plus Best Musical or Comedy, for a sequel Sean admits he needs to rewatch to remember.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Anthony Hopkins nominated for a Sony Pictures Classics drama neither host has managed to see yet — Amanda's new 'Have You Seen the Wife?' stand-in.

segments.heading: Mark Harris on Awards Season and ‘Mike Nichols: A Life’

films.title: Minari
films.note: Harris flags its 'miscategorization' into Best Foreign Language Film as one of the year's real outrages, alongside I May Destroy You getting ignored entirely.

films.title: I Care a Lot
films.note: Harris calls it 'quite a funny movie' with a good Rosamund Pike performance, but still invisible to general audiences as an un-landed Netflix title.

films.title: The Graduate
films.note: Harris wrote about it once before, in Pictures at a Revolution, and was nervous to cover it again — 'a strange thing' to revisit.

films.title: Catch-22
films.note: Nichols' first unlimited-budget project — he only realized late in the process that the material didn't suit his kind of comedy.

films.title: The Day of the Dolphin
films.note: Harris reads it as a cautionary tale about saying yes without thinking it through.

films.title: The Fortune
films.note: Taught Nichols the danger of a fun set producing a bad movie, and of a script he didn't fully believe in — rare for a director Harris calls 'the most writer-friendly of all major American directors.'

films.title: Carnal Knowledge
films.note: Harris insists this one is actually a success that belongs in the 'Mike Nichols pantheon,' not lumped in with the decade's flops — still 'an electric jolt' fifty years later.

films.title: Wolf
films.note: Nichols trying to make 'one for them, one for me' in a single movie — the werewolf blockbuster and the publishing-industry comedy visibly pulling against each other on screen.

films.title: Regarding Henry
films.note: The one that stumped Harris most while researching — producer Scott Rudin told him flatly, 'there's nothing of Mike in this movie.'

films.title: Heartburn
films.note: Harris's pick for the most wrongly maligned film in Nichols' catalog — buried under press about the Ephron marriage it was based on, and the source of the now-memed carbonara-in-bed scene.

films.title: Shock Corridor
films.note: Harris's 'last great thing' — a Sam Fuller asylum thriller he'd somehow never seen until the night before taping, and was 'flabbergasted' by.

films: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
